text: they took hold of Ronin’s arms and lifted him from Dozer. Static bot slung Ronin’s arm over his shoulders . On the edge of Ronin’s optical field, Dozer lurched forward, grabbing the Reg by the throat. Reverting his knees to normal function, Ronin wrapped his arm fully around static bot’s neck, clasped his wrist with his opposite hand, and squeezed . A peal of static rose from the bot’s voice modulator as it was crushed. Its head drooped to the side, the internal support structure of its neck shattered. Ronin shifted his hold on it, tearing through the back of the bot’s shirt and the synthetic skin beneath. He jammed his fingers beneath the lip of the exposed panel and tore off the cover plate . Static bot struggled, fully aware of what was happening — crippled, but functional. It slammed its flailing arms back into Ronin, who raised his own arm to protect his head as he gripped the energy cell and ripped it out of the compartment . Stilling abruptly, the bot’s arms and head sagged, though it remained standing. Ronin turned to Dozer . She stood over Reg’s prone form. His back casing had been torn open as easily as Ronin had torn static bot’s shirt, the metal shielding bent to the point of breaking at the folds. Cables and wires jutted from his neck; his head, with Warlord’s symbol in blood red on the exposed undercasing, lay ten feet away . Dozer met Ronin’s gaze and tossed the power cell onto the motionless bot at her feet . “Didn’t even get to — ” Ronin’s attention shifted before she finished, moving to the Clinic’s entrance. A figure approached the doors from within, features obscured by the reflections of the outside lights. The synth who worked at the desk, most likely, coming to investigate the commotion. But some hidden process, likely developed over so many years of surviving the Dust, told him it wasn’t Mercy . He dropped to a knee, taking hold of static bot’s rifle and lifting it quickly enough that its strap broke off at the barrel. Grasping the foregrip, he pulled back the bolt handle to ensure a round was chambered and raised the stock to his shoulder as the doors slid open . The only thing familiar about the bot who stepped out was the symbol painted on its chest casing . Microseconds ticked by. Twisting toward Dozer, the gearhead fired his rifle from the hip. The first shot was thunderous in the relative silence . Ronin squeezed the trigger as a second round burst from the gearhead’s rifle. Ronin’s shot punched through the bot’s cranial casing, snapping is head to the side. His actuators adjusted to the recoil, sending the next bullet through the gearhead’s left optical receptor . Sparks spraying from its damaged casing, the gearhead spun to face Ronin . Gunshots rang out from the east and west as Ronin fired three more rounds into the gearhead’s torso; nine holes opened up on it in total. It staggered backward, shattering the front doors. One of its legs gave out and it slumped to the ground . Dozer leapt forward. Bracing a foot against the gearhead’s chest, she grabbed the wrist of its gun arm and tore the entire limb off. She tossed it aside — weapon still in its clutch — and lifted her foot. Her boot slammed back down hard, collapsing the gearhead’s torso . With an electric pop, the gearhead stilled . “Shit,” Dozer muttered . The other soldiers moved forward from the corners, footfalls loud over the concrete . Ronin stood up, rifle secure against his shoulder, and approached Dozer. As he passed the entrance, he saw the synth nurse, Mercy, standing just beyond the interior doors . “How are you holding up, Dozer?” he asked, weapon directed at the Clinic . “Clipped a power conduit,” she replied. “Got a power leak. Could be worse .” “We can repair that here.” Mercy didn’t move from her spot, and didn’t look away from Ronin . “Are there any more of them in there?” Ronin scanned the reception area behind her, but detected no movement . “No. He was the only one who came in for repairs last night. There will be a lot more of them here soon, though .” “That’s the plan,” Ronin said. He glanced over his shoulder as Maul and Dodge arrived, the second fire team close behind them. They all held their weapons at the ready . “Situation?” Maul stepped over the deactivated bot in the entryway, boots crunching on broken glass, and handed Ronin his rifle from the base . “Gonna need a repair later, Sarge, but she says the place is clear,” Dozer replied . “We’re not going to have much time,” Ronin said. He slung the base rifle over his shoulder, keeping the weapon he’d picked up from the gearhead. It would do until it was dry. He approached Mercy, lowering the barrel . “You understand what you’re doing?” she asked. “The potential cost of it ?” He nodded and glanced over his shoulder as the remainder of Alpha team approached. Gently, Ronin placed a hand on Mercy’s arm and guided her aside. Together, they watched the soldiers file into the reception area. The space — which had at one time seemed overly large, given the limited staff and functionality of the facility — was soon overcrowded. The temperature increased three degrees . In Cheyenne, an alarm wailed, echoing through the early morning air . “We need to move fast, people,” Maul said. “Defensive positions, weapons free. We don’t know how many are going to come or what they’ll hit us with, but we will not let them through these doors. Understood ?” This wasn’t a low-risk patrol around the base or a scrap run that might encounter a few reavers . This was war . The soldiers replied in unison, voices strong. “Yes, Sergeant !” “Which way to the
